title: Psalms 119
subtitle: Your Word Is a Lamp for My Feet

sections.header: Verses 1–88 — “Blessed Are Those Whose Ways Are Blameless” (Aleph–Kaph)

heb.paragraph: The longest chapter in the Bible: 176 verses. An acrostic: 22 stanzas of 8 verses each, one for each Hebrew letter. Every verse contains a synonym for God’s word (law, statutes, precepts, decrees, commands, word, promise, ordinances). The psalm is a sustained meditation on Torah’s sufficiency. Key verses: "How can a young person stay on the path of purity? By living according to your word" (v.9). "I have hidden your word in my heart that I might not sin against you" (v.11). "Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things in your law" (v.18). "Your word is a lamp for my feet, a light on my path" (v.105). "The unfolding of your words gives light; it gives understanding to the simple" (v.130).

notes.note: "Blessed are those whose ways are blameless, who walk according to the law of the LORD. Blessed are those who keep his statutes and seek him with all their heart." The double beatitude: blameless way + whole-hearted seeking.

"How can a young person stay on the path of purity? By living according to your word. I have hidden your word in my heart." The youth question answered: the word hidden internally produces purity externally.
"Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things in your law." The prayer every Bible student should pray: open my eyes. The wonders are IN the law; we need opened eyes to see them.
"Before I was afflicted I went astray, but now I obey your word." Affliction as pedagogy: suffering teaches obedience. The school of pain produces the student of the word.
"It was good for me to be afflicted so that I might learn your decrees." The hardest sentence in the Psalter to accept: affliction was GOOD. Not in itself but in what it produced.

refs.note: Jesus affirms the Law's permanence and calls disciples to exceed scribal righteousness.
Paul delights in God's law in his inner being, echoing the psalmist's Torah love.
Joshua is commanded to meditate on the Law day and night for success.

hist.context: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the Bible—176 verses in 22 stanzas, one for each Hebrew letter. Each stanza's 8 verses begin with that letter, creating an elaborate acrostic celebrating Torah. The psalm uses eight near-synonyms for God's revelation: law, statutes, precepts, decrees, commands, word, ordinances, and ways. This first half (Aleph through Kaph) establishes the psalmist as an oppressed faithful one who finds life and guidance in Scripture.

sections.header: Verses 89–176 — “Your Word Is a Lamp for My Feet” (Lamedh–Tav)

heb.paragraph: "Your word is a lamp (nēr) for my feet, a light on my path" (v.105). The psalm’s most famous verse. Not a spotlight illuminating the whole road but a lamp for the FEET — the next step. "I am laid low in the dust; preserve my life according to your word" (v.25). "My soul is consumed with longing for your laws at all times" (v.20). "I have more insight than all my teachers, for I meditate on your statutes" (v.99). "How sweet are your words to my taste, sweeter than honey to my mouth!" (v.103). "Great peace have those who love your law, and nothing can make them stumble" (v.165). The psalm ends: "I have strayed like a lost sheep. Seek your servant, for I have not forgotten your commands" (v.176). The final word: a lost sheep who remembers the word.

notes.note: "Your word, LORD, is eternal; it stands firm in the heavens. Your faithfulness continues through all generations." The cosmic permanence of God’s word: eternal, firm, unshaken.
"How sweet are your words to my taste, sweeter than honey! Your word is a lamp for my feet, a light on my path." Taste (sweetness) + sight (lamp/light). The word engages every sense.
"The unfolding of your words gives light; it gives understanding to the simple." Unfolding: as the scroll opens, light emerges. Understanding is not for the clever but for the simple who receive.
"Great peace have those who love your law, and nothing can make them stumble." The peace promise: love of Torah produces shalom. Nothing causes stumbling. Total stability through the word.

"I have strayed like a lost sheep. Seek your servant, for I have not forgotten your commands." The psalm’s last verse is confession: I have strayed. But also: I remember. The lost sheep still knows the shepherd’s voice.

refs.note: The law revives the soul and gives light—themes amplified throughout Psalm 119.
Scripture equips for every good work, fulfilling the psalm's vision of Torah sufficiency.
The living, active word discerns heart and mind as the psalmist's lamp and light.

hist.context: The second half (Lamedh through Tav) includes the famous 'Your word is a lamp for my feet' (v105). The psalmist faces persecution yet clings to God's testimonies as his heritage forever. The acrostic form itself teaches: learning the alphabet of faith means internalizing Scripture from A to Z. Post-exilic Judaism treasured this psalm as the supreme expression of Torah piety, recited in sections throughout the liturgical year.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The cosmic permanence of God’s word: eternal, firm, unshaken…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen I would not be put to shame when I consider all your commands.ESVThen I shall not be put to shame, having my eyes fixed on all your commandments.NIVBlessed are those whose ways are blameless, who walk according to the law of the Lord.ESVBlessed are those whose way is blameless, who walk in the law of the LORD!

rec.text: ""Blessed are those whose ways are blameless, who walk according to the law of the LORD. Blessed are those who keep his statutes and seek him with all their heart." The double beatitude: blameless way …"

rows.label: The Lamp — 119:89–176
rows.text: "Your word is a LAMP for my feet." "Sweeter than honey." "Great PEACE." "I have strayed — seek your servant."

hebtext.note: The longest chapter in the Bible: 176 verses. An acrostic: 22 stanzas of 8 verses each, one for each Hebrew letter. Every verse contains a synonym for God’s word (law, statutes, precepts, decrees, commands, word, promise, ordinances). The psalm is a sustained meditation on Torah’s sufficiency. Key verses: "How can a young person stay on the path of purity? By living according to your word" (v.9). "I have hidden your word in my heart that I might not sin against you" (v.11). "Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things in your law" (v.18). "Your word is a lamp for my feet, a light on my path" (v.105). "The unfolding of your words gives light; it gives understanding to the simple" (v.130).

"Your word is a lamp (nēr) for my feet, a light on my path" (v.105). The psalm’s most famous verse. Not a spotlight illuminating the whole road but a lamp for the FEET — the next step. "I am laid low in the dust; preserve my life according to your word" (v.25). "My soul is consumed with longing for your laws at all times" (v.20). Seek your servant, for I have not forgotten your commands" (v.176). The final word: a lost sheep who remembers the word.

themes.note: Holiness peaks: 176 verses of Torah devotion. The longest chapter in the Bible. The supreme word-of-God meditation. Faith: hidden word, opened eyes. Worship: sweetness, light, peace. Covenant: Torah as the covenant document.
